showFullCode.title: Show Complete File
showFullCode.description: Show complete file for code, with bootstrap and error handling included.

bitly_url.description: The unique bit.ly URL for this GIF

create_datetime.description: The date this GIF was added to the GIPHY database.

embded_url.description: A URL used for embedding this GIF

featured_tags.description: An array of featured tags for this GIF (Note: Not available when using the Public Beta Key)

import_datetime.description: The creation or upload date from this GIF's source.

rating.description: The MPAA-style rating for this content. Examples include Y, G, PG, PG-13 and R

slug.description: The unique slug used in this GIF's URL

source.description: The page on which this GIF was found

source_post_url.description: The URL of the webpage on which this GIF was found.

source_tld.description: The top level domain of the source URL.

tags.description: An array of tags for this GIF (Note: Not available when using the Public Beta Key)

trending_datetime.description: The date on which this gif was marked trending, if applicable.

update_datetime.description: The date on which this GIF was last updated.

url.description: The unique URL for this GIF

username.description: The username this GIF is attached to, if applicable

frames.description: The number of frames in this GIF.

height.description: The height of this GIF in pixels.

mp4.description: The URL for this GIF in .MP4 format.

mp4_size.description: The size in bytes of the .MP4 file corresponding to this GIF.

size.description: The size of this GIF in bytes.

url.description: The publicly-accessible direct URL for this GIF.

webp.description: The URL for this GIF in .webp format.

webp_size.description: The size in bytes of the .webp file corresponding to this GIF.

width.description: The width of this GIF in pixels.

Meta.title: Meta
Meta.description: The Meta Object contains basic information regarding the request, whether it was successful, and the response given by the API.  Check `responses` to see a description of types of response codes the API might give you under different cirumstances.

response_id.description: A unique ID paired with this response from the API.

Pagination.title: Pagination
Pagination.description: The Pagination Object contains information relating to the number of total results available as well as the number of results fetched and their relative positions.

User.title: User
User.description: The User Object contains information about the user associated with a GIF and URLs to assets such as that user's avatar image, profile, and more.

avatar_url.description: The URL for this user's avatar image.

banner_url.description: The URL for the banner image that appears atop this user's profile page.

display_name.description: The display name associated with this user (contains formatting the base username might not).

profile_url.description: The URL for this user's profile.

twitter.description: The Twitter username associated with this user, if applicable.

username.description: The username associated with this user.

Images.title: Images
Images.description: An object containing data for various available formats and sizes of this GIF.

Type.title: Type
Type.description: Type of the gif. By default, this is almost always gif

Official Giphy Documentation: [https://developers.giphy.com/docs/](https://developers.giphy.com/docs/)

The generated code uses the Newtonsoft Json.NET NuGet Package. If the automatic NuGet package restore is enabled, these dependencies will be installed automatically. Therefore, you will need internet access for build.
* Open the solution (GiphyApi.sln) file.
Invoke the build process using Ctrl + Shift + B shortcut key or using the Build menu as shown below.
The build process generates a portable class library, which can be used like a normal class library. More information on how to use can be found at the MSDN Portable Class Libraries documentation.

The supported version is **.NET Standard 2.0**. For checking compatibility of your .NET implementation with the generated library, [click here](https://dotnet.microsoft.com/en-us/platform/dotnet-standard#versions).
The following section explains how to use the GiphyApi.Standard library in a new project.
For starting a new project, right click on the current solution from the solution explorer and choose `Add -> New Project`.

Nodes.AlternateText: Add a new project in Visual Studio

Nodes.Text: Next, choose `Console Application`, provide `TestConsoleProject` as the project name and click OK.

Nodes.AlternateText: Create a new Console Application in Visual Studio

Nodes.Text: The new console project is the entry point for the eventual execution. This requires us to set the `TestConsoleProject` as the start-up project. To do this, right-click on the `TestConsoleProject` and choose `Set as StartUp Project` form the context menu.

In order to use the `GiphyApi.Standard` library in the new project, first we must add a project reference to the `TestConsoleProject`. First, right click on the `References` node in the solution explorer and click `Add Reference...`

Next, a window will be displayed where we must set the `checkbox` on `GiphyApi.Standard` and click `OK`. By doing this, we have added a reference of the `GiphyApi.Standard` project into the new `TestConsoleProject`.

Steps.Title: Add reference of the library project

Nodes.Text: Once the `TestConsoleProject` is created, a file named `Program.cs` will be visible in the solution explorer with an empty `Main` method. This is the entry point for the execution of the entire solution. Here, you can add code to initialize the client library and acquire the instance of a Api class. Sample code to initialize the client library and using Api methods is given in the subsequent sections.
The following parameters are configurable for the API Client:

Data:
Action delegate that configures the HTTP client by using the HttpClientConfiguration.Builder for customizing API call settings.
*Default*: `new HttpClient()`
Represents the logging configuration builder for API calls
The Credentials Setter for Custom Query Parameter

Nodes.Text: The API client can be initialized as follows:
Code-Based Initialization
Configuration-Based Initialization
See the [Configuration-Based Initialization]($h/__additional_documentation/Configuration/Configuration-Based%20Initialization) section for details.

This API uses the following authentication schemes.
* [`api_key (Custom Query Parameter)`]($h/__auth_CustomQueryAuthentication)
Documentation for accessing and setting credentials for api_key.
**Note:** Auth credentials can be set using `CustomQueryAuthenticationCredentials` in the client builder and accessed through `CustomQueryAuthenticationCredentials` method in the client instance.
You must provide credentials in the client as shown in the following code snippet.

* [Gifs]($e/gifs)
* [Stickers]($e/stickers)
An instance of the `GifsApi` class can be accessed from the API Client.
GifsApi gifsApi = client.GifsApi;

Nodes.Text: Sdk Client initialization through configuration.
The SDK client can also be initialized directly from an ```IConfiguration``` instance using the ```FromConfiguration``` method available on the ```Builder``` or ```SDKClient``` class.
This enables the SDK to automatically read configuration values from various sources such as JSON files, environment variables, or other .NET configuration providers.
If a required environment variable or configuration value is missing, the SDK will fall back to its default configuration value.
Example: Initializing SDK Client from Configuration
Nodes.SuggestedLinkText: Example: Initializing SDK Client from Configuration

Nodes.Text: The following code sample demonstrates how to initialize the SDK client using an ```IConfiguration``` section.

The ```Builder.FromConfiguration``` method reads values from the provided configuration section and returns a builder instance, allowing you to override specific properties directly in code if needed before building the final client.
Example Configuration File

Nodes.Text: The builder for logging configuration. Create instance using `LogBuilder.Build()`
Methods

Data: Sets the implementation of Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.ILogger for logging. **The default implementation is ConsoleLogger.**
Sets the Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.LogLevel, which defines logging severity levels. **The default value is LogLevel.Information.**
Sets the global setting to mask sensitive HTTP headers in both requests and responses before logging, safeguarding confidential data. **The default value is True.**
Sets the request logging configuration with the provided builder action. **By default, request logging is enabled, excluding both the body and headers.**
Sets the response logging configuration with the provided builder action. **By default, response logging is enabled, excluding both the body and headers.**

Nodes.Text: Usage Example
Client Initialization with Custom Logger
To use a custom logger, you can provide any implementation of Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.ILogger directly during SDK client initialization.
The builder for request logging configuration. Create instance using `LogRequestBuilder.Build()`
Methods

Data: Sets the logging of the request body. **By default, the body is not logged.**
Sets the logging of the request headers. **By default, the headers are not logged.**
Sets the inclusion of query parameters in the logged request path. **By default, query parameters aren't included in the request path.**
Sets the headers that will exclusively be included in the log output of the request headers. **By default, all headers are included.**
Sets the headers that will be excluded in the log output of the request headers. **By default, no headers are excluded.**
Sets the headers that will be unmasked in the log output of the request headers. **By default, no headers are unmasked.**

Nodes.Text: The builder for response logging configuration. Create instance using `LogResponseBuilder.Build()`

Data: Sets the logging of the response body. **By default, the body is not logged.**
Sets the logging of the response headers. **By default, the headers are not logged.**
Sets the headers that will exclusively be included in the log output of the response headers. **By default, all headers are included.**
Sets the headers that will be excluded in the log output of the response headers. **By default, no headers are excluded.**
Sets the headers that will be unmasked in the log output of the response headers. **By default, no headers are unmasked.**

Nodes.Title: How quickly can we launch our Developer Portal?

Nodes.Text: You can generate and publish your Developer Portal in minutes. Simply upload your OpenAPI specification, customize the look and feel to match your brand, and publish instantly - either as a standalone site or embedded into your existing Developer Portal.

Nodes.Title: How does the Portal stay current as our API evolves?

Nodes.Text: APIMatic automatically updates your portal when your API specification changes. You can re-import updated API definitions, and the portal will regenerate with the latest endpoints, parameters, and documentation while preserving your customizations.

Nodes.Title: What level of customization and control do we have?

Nodes.Text: You can completely customize the look, feel and behavior of your Developer Experience Portal, including primary colors, themes, custom sections, navigation links, custom URL slugs, and branding. You can also choose to host it on your own custom domain or embed it seamlessly into your existing documentation site using a simple HTML script.

Nodes.Title: Is there a way we can integrate this with our existing Developer Portal?

Nodes.Text: Absolutely. APIMatic provides embeddable portal components that can be seamlessly integrated into your existing documentation sites using a simple HTML script. You can also choose to host the portal separately on your own domain while maintaining your existing portal structure.

Nodes.Title: What AI capabilities does this Developer Portal have?

Nodes.Text: **Context Plugins** let developers connect your API to the AI coding assistant they already use (Cursor, GitHub Copilot, Claude, and more) to search, ask questions, and generate working integration code grounded in your live specification.

The portal also includes a built-in **API Copilot** that answers questions from your API reference and product documentation, and an **AI Assist Menu** that sends any documentation page straight to your preferred AI tool as context with one click.
Automatic **LLMs.txt generation** and per-page Markdown exports make your documentation AI-accessible to tools like Cursor, GitHub Copilot, ChatGPT, and Claude.
